subtopic_detail: Adult DKA initial fluid and insulin
stem:
A 25-year-old man with type 1 diabetes mellitus presents to a metropolitan emergency department with 24 hours of vomiting, polyuria, and progressive lethargy following several days of viral upper respiratory illness. He has stopped his usual insulin glargine 20 units at night and his rapid-acting insulin doses for the past 24 hours because of vomiting and concerns about hypoglycaemia.

On examination he is alert with dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, and slow capillary return at three seconds. There is acetone on his breath and his respiration is deep and slow. He has no fever, no abdominal tenderness, and no localising signs of infection. His weight is 75 kg today. He has no chronic complications of diabetes and his HbA1c three months ago was 7.6 percent. There is no recent travel and no chest pain.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 130, blood pressure 110/70, respiratory rate 28, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees
data_table.Investigations: venous gas pH 7.05, bicarbonate 7 mmol/L (22-28), glucose 32 mmol/L, beta-hydroxybutyrate 6.0 mmol/L, sodium 134 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 4.8 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), creatinine 110 micromol/L (60-110), urea 9 mmol/L (3-8)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first hour of fluid and insulin management?

options.text: 1 litre 0.9 percent saline as a rapid bolus over 30 minutes then maintenance at 100 mL/hour without potassium replacement initially in the first hour
options.rationale: Maintenance-only fluids after a single bolus undertreat the typical 5 to 8 litre deficit of adult DKA. Aggressive replacement is required, although tailored to age and comorbidity. (Source: APEG and ADS Adult DKA Guidelines)
source_refs: APEG and ADS Adult DKA Guidelines

options.text: Insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hour immediately without prior fluid resuscitation and crystalloid only added once the potassium falls below 4.0 mmol/L
options.rationale: Insulin should follow fluid resuscitation by 15 to 30 minutes; insulin without volume expansion risks worsening hypotension and cerebral oedema. (Source: APEG and ADS Adult DKA Guidelines)
source_refs: APEG and ADS Adult DKA Guidelines

options.text: 0.9 percent saline 1 L bolus over one hour with intramuscular insulin 10 units every 4 hours and oral potassium supplementation 2 grams daily
options.rationale: Intramuscular insulin is unreliable in DKA because of poor peripheral perfusion; a continuous intravenous insulin infusion is required after fluid resuscitation. (Source: APEG and ADS Adult DKA Guidelines)
source_refs: APEG and ADS Adult DKA Guidelines

options.text: 5 percent dextrose 1 L at 100 mL/hour and rapid-acting subcutaneous insulin 10 units stat with repeat doses every 2 hours guided by capillary glucose
options.rationale: Dextrose is added only once blood glucose falls below 14 mmol/L to allow ongoing insulin infusion without hypoglycaemia; it is not first-line fluid in early DKA. (Source: APEG and ADS Adult DKA Guidelines)
source_refs: APEG and ADS Adult DKA Guidelines

options.text: 0.9 percent saline 500 mL bolus over 15 to 30 minutes, reassess, then 250 to 500 mL/hour with insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hour started after fluids are flowing
options.rationale: Adult DKA initial management: rapid crystalloid bolus to restore intravascular volume, then ongoing replacement at 250 to 500 mL/hour, with intravenous insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour commenced after fluids are running and potassium has been measured. Add potassium when serum K is below 5.5 mmol/L. (Source: APEG and ADS Adult DKA Guidelines)
source_refs: APEG and ADS Adult DKA Guidelines

explanation.summary: Adult DKA management is fluid first (0.9 percent saline 500 mL to 1 L over 15 to 30 minutes followed by 250 to 500 mL per hour) then intravenous insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour. Add potassium chloride when serum potassium falls below 5.5 mmol/L because total body potassium is depleted despite normal or high initial serum levels. Switch to 5 percent dextrose with saline once blood glucose falls below 14 mmol/L to allow ongoing insulin while resolving ketoacidosis. Identify and treat the precipitant.
key_points: Fluid bolus 500 mL to 1 L 0.9 percent saline over 15-30 min
Ongoing replacement 250-500 mL/hour for total deficit 5-8 L
Insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hour after fluids running
Add potassium when serum K < 5.5 mmol/L
Switch to dextrose when glucose < 14 mmol/L
explanation.pearls: Continue the long-acting basal insulin alongside the insulin infusion to avoid rebound DKA when the infusion is stopped, and overlap subcutaneous rapid-acting insulin with the infusion by 1 to 2 hours during transition.

sources.label: APEG and ADS Adult DKA Guidelines

stem:
A 30-year-old woman with a long-standing history of asthma presents to a metropolitan emergency department with worsening shortness of breath over the past four hours. She is on long-acting beta-agonist and corticosteroid inhaler maintenance therapy. She has not had a hospital admission in the last 12 months but has needed three short oral prednisolone courses in the same period.

On arrival she is talking in single words, sitting upright with shoulder elevation. She has audible wheeze with use of accessory muscles. She has received three back-to-back salbutamol nebulisers (5 mg each) and one ipratropium nebuliser (500 micrograms) in the first 30 minutes. Peak expiratory flow is 35 percent of her best (380 L/min recorded six months ago). She has not yet received corticosteroid. She has no chest pain and no fever. Smoke detector activated cooking on her stove this morning.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 132, blood pressure 128/82, respiratory rate 36, SpO2 91 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees
data_table.Investigations: venous gas pH 7.36, pCO2 38 mmHg, bicarbonate 22 mmol/L, chest radiograph hyperinflated lung fields without consolidation or pneumothorax

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes plus intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg, continuous nebulised salbutamol, and senior medical review
options.rationale: Severe asthma exacerbation with peak flow under 50 percent, SpO2 under 92 percent on air, and inadequate response to back-to-back beta-agonist gets intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes plus systemic corticosteroid (intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg or oral prednisolone 50 mg) and continuous nebulised salbutamol, with senior review. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg of 1:1000 solution into the deltoid muscle and intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g for empirical antibacterial cover
options.rationale: Intramuscular adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis or peri-arrest asthma where conventional therapy has failed; antibiotics are not routine for asthma exacerbation without a bacterial trigger. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Intravenous aminophylline loading dose 5 mg/kg over 30 minutes followed by a continuous infusion at 0.5 mg/kg/hour for ongoing bronchodilation
options.rationale: Aminophylline has narrow therapeutic index and is reserved for severe exacerbations refractory to standard therapy; it is not the next step after first-line salbutamol and ipratropium. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Immediate endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation in the emergency department resuscitation bay with paralysis and sedation
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for impending or actual respiratory arrest with falling conscious state, rising carbon dioxide despite maximum therapy, or exhaustion; this patient still has trial of medical therapy remaining. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Heliox 70:30 helium-oxygen mixture by face mask as primary bronchodilator with continued back-to-back salbutamol nebulisers without further escalation
options.rationale: Heliox is an adjunct in selected centres for refractory cases; the evidence base is limited and it is not the next step in standard escalation in Australia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

explanation.summary: Severe and life-threatening asthma exacerbation is treated with oxygen titrated to SpO2 93 to 95 percent, repeated nebulised salbutamol (with ipratropium for severe exacerbations), systemic corticosteroid (oral prednisolone 50 mg or intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg), and intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes when peak flow remains below 50 percent. Refractory cases escalate to aminophylline, salbutamol infusion, heliox, non-invasive ventilation, or intubation under senior care.
key_points: Severe: PEF < 50 percent, talking words, accessory muscles, HR > 110
Life-threatening: silent chest, SpO2 < 92, exhaustion, normal-rising CO2
Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 20 min when first-line therapy fails
Systemic corticosteroid: prednisolone 50 mg oral or hydrocortisone 200 mg IV
Watch CO2 - rising indicates exhaustion and need for ventilatory support
explanation.pearls: A normal or rising carbon dioxide in a severe asthma exacerbation is a red flag indicating exhaustion and imminent respiratory failure - escalate to senior care and consider intubation rather than continuing the medical ladder.

subtopic_detail: Pulmonary embolism Wells score and imaging strategy
stem:
A 72-year-old woman is reviewed on the orthopaedic ward on day 4 after an elective right total knee replacement. She is taking enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneous prophylaxis. She has had four hours of sudden onset shortness of breath and right-sided pleuritic chest pain.

On examination she is mildly distressed with mild right calf swelling but no overt deep vein thrombosis signs. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations are otherwise unremarkable with normal heart sounds and clear chest. Wells score is calculated: PE more likely than alternative diagnosis (3), HR over 100 (1.5), surgery in last 4 weeks (1.5), no haemoptysis, no malignancy, no previous PE; total score 6 (high probability). The on-call radiologist has called the intern to ask whether a D-dimer is required before CTPA is performed.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 110, blood pressure 124/74, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 94 percent on room air, temperature 37.0 degrees
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia rate 110 without S1Q3T3, chest radiograph clear lung fields without effusion or wedge, troponin within normal limits

lead_in: Which imaging strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Wait for D-dimer result with age-adjusted threshold of 0.72 mg/L (age multiplied by 0.01) and perform CTPA only if above this threshold to limit ionising radiation
options.rationale: Age-adjusted D-dimer is for ruling out PE in low or moderate clinical probability patients. With a Wells score above 4 the pretest probability is too high for D-dimer to safely rule out PE. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: PE)

options.text: Proceed directly to CT pulmonary angiogram without D-dimer because PE likely (Wells > 4) requires imaging-based exclusion regardless of D-dimer result
options.rationale: Wells > 4 (PE likely) skips D-dimer because of inadequate negative predictive value at high pretest probability. CTPA is the first-line imaging in Australia and is required to rule in or out PE. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Perform ventilation-perfusion scan instead of CT pulmonary angiogram given the moderate-to-high pretest probability and current chronic kidney disease concerns
options.rationale: V/Q scan is reserved for renal impairment with low risk of indeterminate scan (clear chest radiograph, young patient) or for contrast allergy; CTPA is first-line in adults without contraindication. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Commence empirical therapeutic enoxaparin 1 mg/kg subcutaneously twice daily and arrange outpatient follow-up at the haematology day unit in 48 hours
options.rationale: Empirical therapeutic anticoagulation can bridge a delay to imaging but does not replace definitive imaging. Outpatient management of suspected PE in a postoperative patient without confirmation is unsafe. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Perform bilateral lower limb compression Doppler ultrasound as initial test and treat only if a deep vein thrombosis is identified on the affected operative limb
options.rationale: Lower limb Doppler is a reasonable adjunct (positive Doppler with high pretest probability can avoid CTPA) but is not the primary test in a high probability patient who can have CTPA. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: The two-tier Wells score for PE stratifies pretest probability as PE likely (over 4 points) or unlikely (4 or fewer). PE likely proceeds directly to CT pulmonary angiogram; PE unlikely has D-dimer with age-adjusted threshold (age multiplied by 10 ng/mL above 50 years, i.e. 0.72 mg/L FEU at 72) and only requires CTPA if D-dimer is above threshold. CTPA is first-line imaging in Australia; V/Q scan is reserved for contrast allergy or significant renal impairment with normal chest radiograph.
key_points: Wells > 4: PE likely, proceed direct to CTPA
Wells <= 4: D-dimer first with age-adjusted threshold over 50 years
Age-adjusted D-dimer threshold: age x 10 ng/mL (or x 0.01 if mg/L FEU)
CTPA is first-line imaging; V/Q for contrast allergy or significant CKD
Lower limb Doppler can confirm DVT and avoid CTPA in high probability cases
explanation.pearls: Age-adjusted D-dimer uses the formula (age in years multiplied by 10 ng/mL FEU) above age 50 - this raises the threshold from a fixed 500 to around 720 at age 72 and avoids unnecessary CTPA in older patients with PE unlikely Wells.

subtopic_detail: SIADH versus cerebral salt wasting after SAH
stem:
A 58-year-old woman is on day 7 after surgical clipping of an anterior communicating artery aneurysm following a subarachnoid haemorrhage. Her serum sodium has fallen from 138 to 124 mmol/L over the past 24 hours. She is mildly drowsy. There has been no new neurological deficit on examination this morning.

Nursing observation shows polyuria of 4 litres over the previous 24 hours. On bedside review her mucous membranes are dry, jugular venous pressure is not visible, and capillary return is two seconds. Her body weight has fallen from 68 kg yesterday to 66 kg today. She is on regular enteral feeds via nasogastric tube providing 1500 mL daily and intravenous 0.9 percent saline at 80 mL/hour. She has had no vomiting and no diarrhoea.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 100, blood pressure 110/65, respiratory rate 18, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees
data_table.Investigations: serum sodium 124 mmol/L (135-145), serum osmolality 258 mosm/kg (275-295), urine sodium 80 mmol/L, urine osmolality 580 mosm/kg, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, weight reduction 2 kg over 24 hours

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Fluid restrict to 800 mL daily, withhold intravenous saline, and arrange demeclocycline 600 mg twice daily for refractory disease over the next 48 hours
options.rationale: The picture is volume-contracted (weight loss, dry mucous membranes, polyuria) rather than euvolaemic, making cerebral salt wasting more likely than SIADH. Fluid restriction would worsen cerebral perfusion. (Source: Australian Stroke Foundation Guidelines)

options.text: Administer 3 percent hypertonic saline 100 mL bolus then continuous infusion at 50 mL/hour and aim for a sodium rise of 10 mmol/L over the next 12 hours
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is reserved for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures, coma). Rapid correction risks central pontine myelinolysis. The current sodium is amenable to careful isotonic replacement. (Source: Australian Stroke Foundation Guidelines)

options.text: 0.9 percent saline 1 L over 1 hour then 200 mL/hour, target sodium rise of 6 to 8 mmol/L over 24 hours, salt tablets 6 g daily if hyponatraemia persists
options.rationale: Cerebral salt wasting after subarachnoid haemorrhage is treated with isotonic saline replacement and salt loading, with the goal of restoring intravascular volume and sodium without exceeding 8 to 10 mmol/L rise per 24 hours (central pontine myelinolysis risk). Fluid restriction is contraindicated in cerebral salt wasting because it worsens cerebral perfusion. (Source: Australian Stroke Foundation Guidelines)

options.text: Demeclocycline 600 mg twice daily as a vasopressin receptor antagonist to allow free water excretion and restore serum sodium gradually toward normal range
options.rationale: Demeclocycline blocks vasopressin action and would worsen polyuria and hypovolaemia in cerebral salt wasting. It is also slow to act and rarely used in acute care. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Tolvaptan 15 mg daily as a vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist with strict fluid restriction to 1 litre per day and daily sodium monitoring on the ward
options.rationale: Tolvaptan is used in euvolaemic or hypervolaemic hyponatraemia (SIADH, heart failure) and is dangerous in hypovolaemic hyponatraemia such as cerebral salt wasting. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Hyponatraemia after subarachnoid haemorrhage is most commonly SIADH (euvolaemic, normal urine output) or cerebral salt wasting (hypovolaemic, high urine output, weight loss). Volume status is the key discriminator since urinary sodium and osmolality overlap. Cerebral salt wasting is treated with isotonic saline replacement plus salt loading; SIADH is treated with fluid restriction and tolvaptan if severe. Correction rate is limited to 8 to 10 mmol/L per 24 hours to avoid central pontine myelinolysis.
key_points: SIADH: euvolaemic, normal weight, treat with fluid restriction
CSW: hypovolaemic, weight loss, polyuria, treat with isotonic saline + salt
Both can have urine sodium > 40 and urine osmolality > 100
Maximum correction 8-10 mmol/L per 24 hours to avoid CPM
Hypertonic saline only for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia
explanation.pearls: Weight loss and polyuria differentiate cerebral salt wasting from SIADH after subarachnoid haemorrhage; the management diverges sharply (fluid replacement vs restriction) so getting the diagnosis right matters more than any single laboratory value.

subtopic_detail: Stanford type A aortic dissection acute management
stem:
A 58-year-old man with long-standing poorly controlled hypertension presents to a metropolitan emergency department with sudden severe central chest pain radiating to the interscapular region. The pain reached maximum intensity within seconds and has been steady for two hours.

On examination he is in distress. There is a difference between his blood pressure measurements in the right arm and left arm of 40 mmHg. He has a soft early diastolic murmur at the left sternal edge. The chest is clear and the heart sounds are otherwise normal. Chest radiograph demonstrates a widened mediastinum measuring 9 cm. CT angiogram of the aorta has confirmed a Stanford type A dissection extending from the aortic root through the arch into the descending thoracic aorta. There is no malperfusion of major branch vessels reported. The cardiothoracic team are aware.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 96, blood pressure right arm 180/100, blood pressure left arm 140/85, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 97 percent on room air, temperature 36.9 degrees
data_table.Investigations: 12-lead ECG sinus rhythm without ST changes, troponin within normal limits, chest radiograph widened mediastinum, CT angiogram Stanford type A dissection

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intravenous labetalol infusion targeting systolic blood pressure below 120 and admit to coronary care for ongoing medical management with antihypertensive review
options.rationale: Coronary care admission and medical management alone are appropriate for uncomplicated type B dissection. Type A is a surgical emergency. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Aspirin 300 mg, clopidogrel 600 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg, and admit to coronary care for management of likely non-ST-elevation acute coronary syndrome
options.rationale: Antiplatelets are contraindicated in aortic dissection; this is dissection, not acute coronary syndrome, despite an overlapping presentation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Intravenous labetalol with intravenous nitroprusside as second-line vasodilator and book elective cardiothoracic surgery in 24 to 48 hours after optimisation
options.rationale: Type A dissection is a surgical emergency with mortality rising 1 to 2 percent per hour without operation. Elective delay is contraindicated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Intravenous labetalol infusion targeting heart rate below 60 and systolic blood pressure below 120, opioid analgesia, and immediate cardiothoracic surgery referral for emergency repair
options.rationale: Type A dissection is a surgical emergency requiring immediate cardiothoracic repair. Pre-operative goals are heart rate under 60 (beta-blocker first to reduce wall stress before any vasodilator) and systolic blood pressure under 120, plus analgesia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Therapeutic heparin infusion and admit to intensive care for monitoring of haemodynamics and trends in renal function and creatinine over 24 hours
options.rationale: Anticoagulation is contraindicated in aortic dissection because it worsens the dissection and any bleeding into the pericardium or pleura. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: Stanford type A aortic dissection involves the ascending aorta and is a surgical emergency with mortality rising at 1 to 2 percent per hour without operation. Initial medical therapy targets heart rate under 60 (beta-blocker first to reduce dP/dt and wall stress) and systolic blood pressure under 120, plus opioid analgesia. Beta-blocker must precede any vasodilator (nitroprusside) because vasodilator-induced reflex tachycardia would worsen dissection. Antiplatelets and anticoagulants are contraindicated. Type B is medical first line unless complicated.
key_points: Type A: ascending aorta, surgical emergency, mortality 1-2 percent per hour
Type B: descending aorta beyond left subclavian, medical first line
Pre-op: HR < 60, SBP < 120, beta-blocker before vasodilator
Antiplatelets and anticoagulants contraindicated
Blood pressure differential > 20 mmHg and widened mediastinum are classical signs
explanation.pearls: Beta-blocker before vasodilator in aortic dissection: nitroprusside alone causes reflex tachycardia that increases the shear stress (dP/dt) on the dissection flap and propagates the tear.

stem:
A 65-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus presents to a metropolitan emergency department with 24 hours of erythema and severe pain in the left thigh after a minor garden scratch. He describes the pain as far worse than the appearance suggests and is unable to bear weight on the affected leg.

On examination he is septic-appearing but alert. The left thigh shows ill-defined erythema extending from the knee to the upper thigh with marked tenderness but no obvious blistering, no clear demarcation, and no crepitus on palpation. The skin surface looks deceptively mild compared with the pain. The general surgical registrar reviews and is reluctant to operate on grounds of insufficient skin signs, suggesting observation and reassessment in 6 hours with empirical antibiotics. The microbiology registrar has been called by the team.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 110, blood pressure 130/80, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 97 percent on room air, temperature 38.4 degrees
data_table.Investigations: white cells 19.0 x10^9/L (4-11), haemoglobin 132 g/L (130-180), sodium 132 mmol/L (135-145), creatinine 110 micromol/L (60-110), glucose 14 mmol/L, CRP 220 mg/L (<5), lactate 2.8 mmol/L, LRINEC score 8 (CRP 4 + WCC 1 + Hb 0 + Na 2 + creatinine 0 + glucose 1)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g daily and observe with reassessment of the leg appearance and inflammatory markers in six hours of close monitoring
options.rationale: LRINEC 8 with pain out of proportion and septic appearance in a diabetic patient is high probability necrotising fasciitis; six-hour observation risks mortality given the documented 9 percent per hour rise in death rate with delayed surgery. (Source: ASID Skin and Soft Tissue Infections)
source_refs: ASID Skin and Soft Tissue Infections

options.text: MRI lower limb with contrast to confirm necrotising fasciitis based on fascial enhancement and fluid collections before booking the operating theatre
options.rationale: MRI may show fascial thickening but is not sensitive enough to rule out necrotising fasciitis and the time to scan delays surgical exploration which is the diagnostic gold standard. (Source: ASID Skin and Soft Tissue Infections)
source_refs: ASID Skin and Soft Tissue Infections

options.text: Punch biopsy of skin sent for frozen section histopathology to confirm fascial necrosis before surgical decision-making is made by the consultant
options.rationale: Frozen section biopsy of skin alone misses subfascial disease and adds a delay; intraoperative finger test on surgical exploration is the diagnostic gold standard. (Source: ASID Skin and Soft Tissue Infections)
source_refs: ASID Skin and Soft Tissue Infections

options.text: Bedside ultrasound looking for fascial thickening and subcutaneous fluid then targeted incisional biopsy if those features are seen on the ultrasound
options.rationale: Bedside ultrasound has poor sensitivity for necrotising fasciitis and a normal scan does not exclude the diagnosis; pursuit of imaging delays definitive surgical exploration. (Source: ASID Skin and Soft Tissue Infections)
source_refs: ASID Skin and Soft Tissue Infections

options.text: Urgent operating theatre for surgical exploration and aggressive debridement with intravenous meropenem, clindamycin, and vancomycin broad spectrum cover
options.rationale: LRINEC 8 in a diabetic patient with pain out of proportion and septic features is high probability necrotising fasciitis. The skin signs lag behind subcutaneous disease and a low threshold for surgical exploration is the standard of care. Empirical antibiotics include broad gram-negative cover (meropenem), antitoxin (clindamycin for streptococcal and clostridial), and gram-positive cover including MRSA (vancomycin). (Source: ASID Skin and Soft Tissue Infections)
source_refs: ASID Skin and Soft Tissue Infections

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis carries 25 to 35 percent mortality even with prompt surgery and approaches 100 percent without it. Skin signs lag behind subcutaneous disease, so pain out of proportion to appearance and systemic toxicity should prompt operative exploration regardless of how the skin looks. LRINEC 8 or more is high risk (PPV around 65 percent) but a lower score does not rule out the diagnosis. Empirical antibiotics are broad spectrum: meropenem (or piperacillin-tazobactam) plus clindamycin for antitoxin effect plus vancomycin for MRSA, then narrowed on culture.
key_points: LRINEC >= 8: high probability; LRINEC < 6 does not rule out
Pain out of proportion + sepsis + diabetes: low threshold to operate
Imaging delays surgery and adds little; the finger test at operation is gold standard
Empirical antibiotics: meropenem + clindamycin + vancomycin
Mortality rises around 9 percent per hour of surgical delay
explanation.pearls: Clindamycin is included in necrotising fasciitis cover for its antitoxin effect rather than its antibacterial spectrum: it switches off streptococcal exotoxin and clostridial toxin production through ribosomal inhibition even when the patient is already on a broader gram-positive agent.

sources.label: ASID Skin and Soft Tissue Infections
